---
title: "Tutorials & case studies"
---

This section collects longer-form explanations, examples, and the case studies presented in the VedicDateTime paper.

---

## Tutorial 1 — Compute Panchanga for a place

A common workflow is:

1. Convert a Gregorian date to a Julian day (`gregorian_to_jd`) or start from a JD.
2. Provide a location as `place <- c(latitude, longitude, timezone_offset)`.
3. Compute Panchanga entities: tithi, vaara, nakshatra, yoga, karana.

Example:

```r
place <- c(15.34, 75.13, +5.5)

jd <- 2459778

tithi(jd, place)
vaara(jd)
nakshatra(jd, place)
yoga(jd, place)
karana(jd, place)
```

---

## Tutorial 2 — Turn Vedic entities into time-series features

VedicDateTime enables calendar features such as:

- tithi index
- nakshatra index
- yoga index
- rashi / masa

A typical workflow is:

1. Compute one or more entity indices for each observation date.
2. Encode indices as factors (or one-hot variables).
3. Use these as regressors in a forecasting / regression model.

```r
# Pseudocode sketch (adapt to your workflow)
# df$tithi <- tithi(df$jd, place)[1]
# df$nakshatra <- nakshatra(df$jd, place)[1]
# model <- lm(y ~ factor(tithi) + factor(nakshatra), data = df)
```

---

## Case study 1 (paper) — Calendar seasonality and economic output

The paper highlights that calendars can have strong effects on economic and social behavior, and discusses India’s **Index of Industrial Production (IIP)** as an example.

Key motivations described:

- India has multiple regions and festivals from multiple religions.
- A single Gregorian calendar representation may be insufficient to model various seasonal components.
- During Diwali, consumer consumption can be higher while industrial output is generally lower.

VedicDateTime is used to convert the time axis and analyze calendar seasonality effects.

---

## Case study 2 (paper) — Diwali regressors (moving festival effects)

The paper also discusses creating regressors around the Diwali period and comparing industrial production patterns pre and post Diwali.

Motivation:

- Moving holidays create shifting impacts in Gregorian time.
- Encoding festival timing in a lunisolar calendar can provide stable features for modeling.

---

## Read the paper

- DOI: https://doi.org/10.1007/s11042-023-16553-w
- Embedded PDF: see the [Publication](publications.qmd) page
